officialSources.title: Learn how to improve Sheets performance
evidence: Google documents background calculations, visible progress while work continues, and edit-triggered recalculation behavior in Sheets.
Google documents performance guidance for repeated expressions, volatile functions, lookup helpers, and conditional formatting.

officialSources.title: Optimize your data references to improve Sheets performance
evidence: Google documents data-reference choices that affect computation speed, stability, CPU usage, and load speed on large datasets.
Google documents closed ranges, avoiding long dependency chains, and reducing cross-spreadsheet import round trips as performance guidance.

officialSources.title: Learn how to optimize your data ingestion
evidence: Google documents different ingestion paths for small copy/paste, import functions, and very large CSV ingestion.
Google documents Connected Sheets and BigQuery as a path when direct Sheets import is too large.

officialSources.title: Tips for improving Excel's performance
evidence: Microsoft documents Excel performance work and guidance across calculation, lookup, memory allocation, copy/paste, undo, scrolling, filtering, file open, and programmability.
Microsoft documents circumstances that affect grid editing, window movement, graphics acceleration, cell editing, monitors, launching, and data preview performance.

officialSources.title: Differences between using a workbook in the browser and in Excel
evidence: Microsoft documents that Excel for the web has browser-specific workbook behavior and feature differences from desktop Excel.
Microsoft documents workbook interactivity, navigation, file-format, and unsupported-feature differences that shape browser responsiveness expectations.

officialSources.title: File size limits for workbooks in SharePoint
evidence: Microsoft documents that large Excel for the web workbooks may not open, edit, or refresh in a browser window when service limits are exceeded.
Microsoft documents Microsoft 365 SharePoint workbook size limits to avoid excessive disk or memory use online.

officialSources.title: Excel specifications and limits
evidence: Microsoft documents workbook, worksheet, calculation, PivotTable, selection, memory, and resource limits that bound large workbook interactions.
Microsoft documents workbook and Data Model scale as memory and system-resource bounded in supported environments.

limitations: This artifact captures official public documentation and published responsiveness guidance, not a live browser or desktop timing run inside Google Sheets or Microsoft Excel.
The comparison proves direct external UI-responsiveness evidence exists; it does not claim bilig is 10x more responsive than Google Sheets or Microsoft Excel.
bilig still needs incumbent p95 frame, edit, selection, paste, filter, and scroll timing artifacts on equivalent corpuses before claiming UI-responsiveness dominance.

dimensions.title: Visible Edit And Calculation Responsiveness
biligEvidence: The large-workbook SLO scorecard checks a 10k browser worker visible edit patch p95 budget.
The headed browser contracts check bounded mutation-to-visible response on the 250k wide workbook corpus.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents background calculation behavior and edit-triggered recalculation guidance for Sheets.
Google documents data-reference patterns that affect computation speed, stability, CPU usage, and load speed.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents performance guidance for grid editing, calculation, lookups, memory, and cell editing.
Microsoft documents calculation and memory limits that affect workbook interaction latency.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has repo-executable visible-edit and mutation-to-visible p95 contracts, while Google and Microsoft publish responsiveness guidance but not matching p95 timing traces for bilig corpuses.
residualLimitations: bilig still needs live Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel timing for the same visible-edit corpuses.
Official docs do not provide controlled p95 edit latency baselines.

dimensions.title: Scroll Selection Filtering Responsiveness
biligEvidence: The headed browser frame-pacing contracts check dense 100k diagonal browse and wide 250k horizontal browse frame p95 budgets.
The dominance scorecard separately requires browser UI responsiveness artifacts instead of treating engine speed as UI proof.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents conditional formatting and open-range modeling choices that can affect large-sheet responsiveness.
Google documents cross-spreadsheet imports and long reference chains as sources of load and calculation delay.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents performance work and tips for scrolling, filtering, copy/paste, undo, cell editing, graphics acceleration, and file open.
Microsoft documents browser-vs-desktop workbook differences that matter to Excel for the web interactions.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has checked frame-pacing contracts for large workbook browsing; incumbent docs document the interaction areas but not a comparable browser-frame benchmark artifact.
residualLimitations: bilig still needs direct Sheets and Excel scroll, selection, paste, filter, and sort timing traces on equivalent workbooks.
The current bilig headed checks do not yet cover every expected spreadsheet interaction mode.

dimensions.title: Browser Workbook Resource Boundaries
biligEvidence: The large-workbook SLO scorecard checks local load, worker warm start, visible patching, and headed frame contracts under committed 100k and 250k corpuses.
The large-workbook scale evidence separately captures official incumbent file and grid boundaries.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google documents ingestion paths and performance guidance for large datasets and imports.
Google documents modeling choices that reduce CPU use and improve stability for large datasets.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft documents online workbook size limits and cases where large workbooks may not open, edit, or refresh in a browser window.
Microsoft documents workbook and Data Model resource limits relevant to large browser workbook responsiveness.
dimensions.comparisonResult: bilig has local browser-worker SLO evidence for current corpuses, while Google and Microsoft publish resource-boundary docs that must shape live comparison runs.
residualLimitations: bilig needs larger hosted-browser corpuses to span more of incumbent resource-boundary behavior.
Official limits do not prove incumbent p95 responsiveness under those limits.

dimensions.title: Live Incumbent UI Timing Gap
biligEvidence: The generated dominance scorecard keeps UI responsiveness partial until direct Google Sheets and Microsoft Excel timing evidence exists.
The SLO scorecard records official-docs comparison evidence but does not allow it to satisfy the live incumbent timing requirement.
googleSheetsEvidence: Google's official docs describe responsiveness-sensitive behavior and performance guidance, not a downloadable p95 UI benchmark.
Google Sheets performance docs are modeling guidance rather than a controlled trace against bilig's checked corpuses.
microsoftExcelEvidence: Microsoft's official docs describe responsiveness-sensitive behavior, performance improvements, and browser differences, not a matching p95 UI benchmark.
Excel performance docs are guidance and product-limit references rather than a controlled trace against bilig's checked corpuses.
dimensions.comparisonResult: The UI-responsiveness evidence gap is narrowed from missing external evidence to missing live incumbent timing, preserving honest 10x claim gating.
residualLimitations: A future live timing artifact must identify browser or desktop version, hardware, tenant/file location, workbook corpus, sample counts, and p95 measurement method.
Until then, this category remains partial evidence rather than a 10x product win.
